issue_title: python tasks2: honor intransitive dependencies
issue_body:
In the new Python pipeline, intransitive dependencies are participating in select_interpreter, test, run and so on.

How to produce:

```
python_library(name='py2_only', compatibility=['CPython<3'])

python_library(name='py3_only', compatibility=['CPython>=3'])

python_binary(name='mytest',
    source='mytest.py',
    dependencies=[ intransitive(':py2_only'), ':py3_only'],
)
```

FAILURE: Unable to detect a suitable interpreter for compatibilities: CPython<3 && CPython>=3 && CPython<3,CPython>=3 (Conflicting targets: pkg:mytest, pkg:py2_only, pkg:py3_only)

Would a PR be helpful here, or the current behavior is intentional/not a bug?

Context on what I'm trying to do: In our current (non-pants) system, we have a package called `base` which is deployed to our pypi twice: as a python2-only wheel and as a python3-only wheel. The difference is that the Python 3 version contains a few additional sources that are using Python 3 specific syntax (like `yield from`) so a universal package is impossible (Python 2 would choke when parsing this file).

On top of that, we have some packages (`type1`) that are universal (the same sources support both Python 2 & Python 3) and depend on the `base` mentioned above. When a binary/test depends on `type1` we know that the right version of `base` will be transitively picked up by pip.

We also have a package `type2`, that like base, has distinct files for Python 2 and Python 3 versions and both of these versions depend on `base`. When a binary/test depends on `type2` we know that the right version of `base` will be transitively picked up by pip.

In pants, I thought to accomplish this by creating a `python_aggregation` target, like this:

```
python_library(
   name='base',
   dependencies=[intransitive(':base_py2'), intransitive(':base_py3')]
)
```

and create a task that runs at bootstrap (similarly to substitute_aliased_targets) that would "hotwire" the right dependency based on the compatibility requirements of the binary/test being run. Thoughts?

issue_title: move glob matching to its own file

We should probably move the glob matching logic into its own file anyway, and that would also resolve this issue (because `expand_single()` doesn't have to be public). @stuhood [mentioned this issue on that PR](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/5769/files/39d9d63cbeb858f60da012d63732eb2876060924#diff-c6c0f72b8c634dad9d8802af249673fe).

issue_title: Kill pantsd-runner instances if their client disconnects
issue_body:
Relates to #5285.

Currently, it's possible to repro a case where if the client disconnects, a `pantsd-runner` instance will hang forever. This might mean that it hangs while locks are held (such as the workspace lock), which will prevent other commands from running.

It should be possible to use a strategy similar to #5285 to resolve this, where the `pantsd-runner` process has a thread watching for the kill signal from the nailgun client, and terminates itself. But there is also a question of why the `pantsd-runner` fully daemonizes itself... if it were to keep the `pantsd` instance as its parent process, and would be terminated by the daemon going away, which would make our lives easier.

issue_title: Port `fs.py` to rust
issue_body:
It has become obvious that our current invalidation granularity (the python function level) is too fine-grained to implement filesystem access efficiently. There seem to be a very good supply of rust libraries to support this type of filesystem access.

Two related improvements would then be to either:
# provide support for writing any Task function in rust instead (very general, but potentially overkill), and then porting most of {{fs.py}} to rust.
# port {{fs.py}} directly to rust as intrinisics, with a reduced public API (ie, one that only supports generating Snapshot objects for PathGlobs).

The former choice seems more general, but I would be concerned that the invalidation tracking overhead would remain a bottleneck, so I'm leaning toward option 2.

issue_title: Consider porting graph-inspection tests to rust
issue_body: There are a series of graph-introspection tests in `tests/python/pants_test/engine/test_scheduler.py` that are half integration test, and half unit test (in that they inspect the internals of the execution graph to validate how a result was achieved). All tests that inspect graph internals have been skipped.

issue_title: Robustify fingerprinting against sparse set collisions.
issue_body: Today, options fingerprinting for one example - may be more examples, proceeds by fingerprinting present options only. This could lead to a case where, given a full set of options, one fp session fingerprints 2 out of 3 and another session a different 2 out of 3, but in both cases with the same values in the same sort order. By neglecting to capture the option key the values we fingerprint are associated with, we are subject to this sparseness collision issue.

issue_title: sockeye gpu locking incompatible with $CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES
issue_body:
It appears that sockeye's default gpu locking behavior is incompatible with `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` being set. Here's an example of two independently scheduled sockeye jobs running on the same machine, with `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` set:

```
# Job 1, cluster assigns GPU 2
export CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=2
python -m sockeye.train .... --device-ids 0
# Sockeye is now running on absolute gpu 2 but it thinks its training on (absolute) gpu 0
# so it puts a global lock on gpu 0 with file /tmp/sockeye.gpu0.lock
# -----
# Job 2 on same machine, separate process, cluster assigns GPU 3
export CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=3
python -m sockeye.train .... --device-ids 0
# Without locking, this sockeye process would grab gpu 3 and work fine
# but it thinks it's grabbing absolute gpu 0 and sees that gpu 0 is locked
# (/tmp/sockeye.gpu0.lock) so it fails.
```
In other words, when `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` is set, sockeye still behaves as if it is receiving an absolute/global gpu id, when in fact the gpu ids have been renumbered locally with respect to that process. This results in a incorrect lock placement.

The current workaround I see is for users who set `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` to also run with the `--disable-device-locking` flag; a nicer fix would involve sockeye device locking checking `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES` to back-compute the absolute gpu id from it's local/relative gpu id, in order to correctly number the global lock. I _think_ it would look something like this:

```
# Example 1:
# local sockeye gpu id: 0
# CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=3
# sockeye places global lock on gpu 3
# -----
# Example 2:
# local sockeye gpu ids: 1,2
# CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=3,5,0,2
# sockeye places global lock on gpus 5 and 0
# -----
# Example 3:
# local sockeye gpu ids: 1,2
# CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES is not set
# sockeye places global lock on gpus 1 and 2
```
Thanks for this nice toolkit!

issue_title: Unicode tokens cause IndexError
issue_body:
When trying to tag an address that has unicode tokens (after tokenization) the resulting clean token in tokenFeatures is an empty string causing an IndexError.
```
>>> import usaddress
>>> usaddress.tag('1 å drive')
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/usaddress/__init__.py", line 84, in tag
    for token, label in parse(address_string) :
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/usaddress/__init__.py", line 73, in parse
    features = tokens2features(tokens)
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/usaddress/__init__.py", line 168, in tokens2features
    token_features = tokenFeatures(token)
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/usaddress/__init__.py", line 141, in tokenFeatures
    features = {'abbrev' : token_clean[-1] == u'.',
IndexError: string index out of range
```

The fix (I think) should be:
```
token_clean = re.sub(r'(^[\W]*)|([^.\w]*$)', u'', token)
```
to
```
 token_clean = re.sub(r'(^[\W]*)|([^.\w]*$)', u'', token, flags=re.UNICODE)
```

issue_title: Suite Number not parsed
issue_body:
When a suite, office, or apartment number is not labeled as such, it is not identified using usaddress.tag().

Example:
ORIGINAL STRING:  14855 Blanco Rd.  #108 None San Antonio TX 78216
PARSED TOKENS:    [(u'14855', 'AddressNumber'), (u'Blanco', 'StreetName'), (u'Rd.', 'StreetNamePostType'), (u'#', 'OccupancyIdentifier'), (u'108', 'AddressNumber'), (u'None', 'StreetName'), (u'San', 'PlaceName'), (u'Antonio', 'PlaceName'), (u'TX', 'StateName'), (u'78216', 'ZipCode')]
UNCERTAIN LABEL:  AddressNumber

When this error is raised, it's likely that either (1) the string is not a valid person/corporation name or (2) some tokens were labeled incorrectly

This will be parsed using usaddress.parse(), however the 'OccupancyIdentifier' will be found as '#'.

issue_title: Parsing of XML nodes without children types
issue_body:
I would like to have the ability in jxmlease to control how XML nodes w/o children are rendered. At the moment, this is done as empty string:

```
>>> x2 = jxmlease.parse("<a/>")
>>> x2
XMLDictNode(xml_attrs=OrderedDict(), value=OrderedDict([(u'a', XMLCDATANode(xml_attrs=OrderedDict(), value=u''))]))
>>> x2.prettyprint()
{u'a': u''}
```

So actually it means, that even <a> has no children nodes, it is associated with an empty string. From an implementation point of view, I would like have the possibility to control how this is rendered. Technically it could be an empty list, empty dict or NoneType as well. Preferred implementation from my point of view would actually be the NoneType:

```
>>> x2 = jxmlease.parse("<a/>")
>>> x2.prettyprint()
{u'a': None}
```

issue_title: Potential for further spatial optimization of generated CDXJ
issue_body:
There exists the potential for further de-duplication of CDXJ entries, particularly from multiple capture sources. Two WARCs, with entries containing headerT1/payloadX and headerT2/payloadX could potentially be merged, as the payload/response body is the same.

This might also have ramifications of squashing important information that resides in the headers that cause browsers to behave differently on replay based on which header is served.

This should be investigated further and provided as an option.

Credit to @b5 for suggestion.

issue_title: Use ipfs.get instead of ipfs.cat
issue_body:
In both the ipwb replay and indexer we use the `cat()` function for IPFS via the `ipfsapi` module to obtain data from ipfs within Python. Would it be better to use `ipfsapi.get()`? Can we `.get()` and return the data from a function? The docs seem to want a path for the data to be stored whereas we want to keep it in memory to mitigate disk i/o.

An advantage might be that `.get()` appears to have built-in compression, at least from the ipfs binary. I have yet to check to verify that this functionality is available in the `ipfsapi` python module.

The `cat()` is meant for display, not retrieval per the ipfs `--help` docs.

issue_title: googleads examples: if 'entries' in page check is not suitable to check for results
issue_body:
in many of the googleads examples, eg. get_campaigns.py there is a 

```python
if 'entries' in page:
    print(something)
else:
    print('No campaigns were found.')
```

However, the result of a request not finding data looks like:
    {
        'totalNumEntries': 0,
        'Page.Type': 'CampaignPage',
        'entries': []
    }

So there is always an 'entries' key. and else is never reached.
It would be better to have instead:
```python
if page['totalNumEntries'] > 0:
```

issue_title: Parsing a 504 error
issue_body:
Hey guys,
Sometimes we receive a HTTP 504 response error that is not appropriately being caught. In `parse_response` when you call [`payload = resp.json()`](https://github.com/lob/lob-python/blob/master/lob/api_requestor.py#L25), it will throw a `ValueError("No JSON object could be decoded")` error.

What I'm thinking of adding before `payload = resp.json()`:
```python
if resp.status_code == 504:
    raise error.APIConnectionError(resp.content,
        resp.content, resp.status_code, resp)
```

Thoughts? Happy to submit a PR if you think this is good.

issue_title: Move girder_io and docker plugin code out of girder_worker.core
issue_body:
### Deliverables:
+ Maintain backward compatibility with girder_worker.run
+ Allow custom tasks to leverage girder_io and docker functionality.
### Technical Notes:
+ girder_io input/output type becomes a shim for library stored outside of girder_worker.core
+ docker exec and plugin become a shim for library stored outside of girder_worker.core
+ Not all tasks that want to do I/O with girder,  or run docker tasks will fit perfectly into the docker_run paradigm (e.g.  conditionally run one docker container over another).  Rather than overloading docker_run() with many many many options,  allow custom packages to implement their own task that uses the basic mechanics of docker and girder I/O. 
### Depends on: None

issue_title: Why does making JSON predictions require a Predictor?
issue_body: We now have a `--use-dataset-reader` flag.  It seems like this is pretty much always what someone would want to use if they are running things from the command line.  If you look at the code, using `--use-dataset-reader` means you go to `Predictor.predict_instances`, which has a default implementation in the base class (the only subclass implementations add stuff that's specific to the demo).  We don't need a `Predictor` at all to handle this use case.  #1674 implements a `Predictor` for the sole purpose of making JSON predictions, but the code just re-implements dataset reader logic, and it would almost certainly be used with a `--use-dataset-reader` flag anyway.  So why do we require a `Predictor` for running `allennlp predict`?  It seems like we only really need it for demos.

issue_title: Customize Results for GetFieldChoices view
issue_body:
Are there any best practices around how to further limit the results of the `GetFieldName` view? In my particular use case, all of the objects in my system belong to an "account" via foreign key, and I would like to limit the choices to include only those with the same "account" as the filterset being created. For reference, I am looking to implement something similar to the "forwarding" feature of Django Autocomplete Light.

https://django-autocomplete-light.readthedocs.io/en/master/tutorial.html?highlight=forwarded

The idea being that once the "account" is selected in the filterset, it would be forwarded to the `GetFieldChoices` view, and then used to further limit the choices returned.

I am messing around with trying to implement this myself, but just wanted to see if anybody could offer some guidance or best practices.

issue_title: text/plain sources are not parsed as utf8
issue_body:
When loading a package from a `text/plain` source, its encoding is assumed to be 'iso-8859-1' (latin) instead of the standard 'utf-8'.
```
$ curl -vv <redacted>.json | od -a -tx2
...
< HTTP/1.1 200 OK
...
< Content-Type: text/plain
...
0000000    {   "   n   a   m   e   "   :   "   t   e   s   t   3   "   ,
             227b    616e    656d    3a22    7422    7365    3374    2c22
0000020    "   t   i   t   l   e   "   :   "   R   �   �   u   n   i   o
             7422    7469    656c    3a22    5222    a9c3    6e75    6f69
```
This is proper UTF8 for the letter “é”.

On the other hand,
```
>>> from datapackage import Package
p=Package('https://s3.amazonaws.com/rawstore.datahub.io/f539d44e5e88aa895f3dcff4a46d2ba6.json')
>>> p.descriptor
{'name': 'test3', 'title': 'RÃ©union', 'resources': [{'path': 'test.csv', 'pathType': 'local', 'name': 'test', 'format': 'csv', 'mediatype': 'text/csv', 'encoding': 'UTF-8', 'dialect': {'delimiter': ',', 'quoteChar': '"'}, 'schema': {'fields': [{'name': 'bar', 'type': 'integer', 'format': 'default'}, {'name': 'kel', 'type': 'integer', 'format': 'default'}, {'name': 'zhur', 'type': 'integer', 'format': 'default'}, {'name': 'tur', 'type': 'string', 'format': 'default'}], 'missingValues': ['']}, 'profile': 'data-resource'}], 'profile': 'data-package’}
``` 
is garbled.

issue_title: Implement support for sending external position update
issue_body: We should add the external position message implemented by bitcraze/crazyflie-firmware#147 in the lib to allow python programs to send to the Crazyflie its current measured position (for example to implement a camera-base Crazyflie tracking).

issue_title: Add a group_by function
issue_body:
Use case:

Given

[
{"type":"large","ip":"10.0.0.1"},
{"type":"large","ip":"10.0.0.2"},
{"type":"small","ip":"10.0.0.3"}
]
I'd like to create:

{
  "large": [{"type":"large","ip":"10.0.0.1"},{"type":"large","ip":"10.0.0.2"}],
  "small":[{"type":"small","ip":"10.0.0.3"}]
}

Is there any query expression to do it?

Thanks very much.

issue_title: pyiso doesn't work well behind http proxy
issue_body:
Hello 
I find many cases that if the python is behind a corporate proxy, then pyiso cannot work because there is no way to pass down the Proxy Setting (session attribute) into base.request().
Thank you

issue_title: Rewrite components in Go so they are resilient, testable, and self-publishing
issue_body:
There's been some discussion and debate in #2701 about how to approach refactoring components. The core maintainers had an architectural meeting today and decided to move forward with a Go-based rewrite of the components, with the following goals:
- ~~No more Docker-in-Docker for the builder component -- instead, bind-mount the host's Docker volume so we can use that Docker graph~~ WONTFIX: This introduces security issues (breakouts in container builds can affect the host OS)
- Better log output for components, showing the component's actual status and helpful details when it fails (no more `waiting for confd to write initial templates` as in #2906)
- Components are more easily testable and less fragile (such as builder - see #3578)
- confd runs in-memory, enabling us to vendor some of its code so we can use native templating logic
- Shared Go-based libraries (with unit tests!) for code common among boot scripts
- Boot logic in a component is minimal thanks to the previous point ^
- Cleaner code and improved control flow made possible by use of Go idioms and channels (for example, rewriting the SSL certificate generation logic)

A long-term goal here is to at some point in the future be able to say `docker run -P deis/builder:2.0` and have the component come up and publish itself with everything it needs self-contained.

**Implementation details/questions**
- Do we use go-extpoints or not? An argument against is that it lends itself well to plugins, but that may not be as useful in this implementation

**Bonus**
It would be great to address the following as part of this refactor:
- nginx template rewrite (#3536)
- better handling of Ceph OSD creation errors (#2519)

replaces #2701

issue_title: Connection Draining
issue_body: Deis currently supports sending a sigterm to the running container and giving it 10 seconds to clean up.  It would be nice to have the ability to have deis wait for the container's connection to close before forcefully shutting down the container.

issue_title: Stop after doc tests for pure docs PRs
issue_body: If a PR only affects documentation, there's no need to proceed with unit tests or integration tests. Ideally, Jenkins could determine this based on the files affected by the commits and only run docs tests.

issue_title: Router and application tagging
issue_body:
We're considering running a Deis cluster to host a bunch of services in geographically-separated regions.  This is mostly for data-location-compliance, but also plays a major role in application speed.

While the tags will allow us to schedule apps onto specific hosts (grouped by region), everything still travels through the same router, which negates the speed and bring the compliance into question.

Would it be possible to run multiple routers with tags, which can then only route apps with the appropriate tags?

For instance: 
www.example.com -> uk.deis.example.com -> UK router -> UK hosts, and 
www.example.net -> us.deis.example.com -> US router -> US hosts

The alternative seems to be running multiple clusters, but then we're looking at 4x everything and additional client-side complexity.  Ideally an app-level config should set the region and everything flows from there.

(For clarity: IP-level restrictions wouldn't help here -- all apps are still publicly accessible, but we wouldn't pass data through a region unless the user or host is actually there.)

issue_title: Respect output format for error presentation whenever possible
issue_body:
@bd4 I want us to discuss this a little bit.

I think it makes a lot of sense that `globus transfer ls --format json --endpoint-id 'badepid'` would produce a JSON error message.

However, there are a number of conditions under which we do not receive JSON from the other end of the connection.
For example, the web server may respond with a raw body of `500 Internal Server Error`.
In those cases, should we wrap this in a standard JSON container, like `{"message": "500 Internal Server Error"}` ?

To me, the target use case is stuff like nonexistent endpoints, which the caller may want to parse and inspect.
They should be able to, _relatively_ safely, hand stderr to something that consumes JSON.

I think that would be totally acceptable, but I'm not sure I've thought through all of the options.
There are errors that we can't handle so easily in this way, namely Python stacktraces.
I don't want to be JSONifying every exception ever thrown, just to slavishly obey the request for `--format JSON`

Where do we draw the line? Do we say "We will convert any error responses from the APIs to JSON." ?
If we can't crystalize this into a simple statement, we probably don't have clear enough thinking about it yet.

issue_title: Rename `bookmark locate` to `bookmark resolve`
issue_body:
And improve the helptext.

We agreed that it makes more sense to talk about "resolving a bookmark to a path".

issue_title: Remove Deprecated Transfer Helpers
issue_body:
We have officially deprecated `make_submit_transfer_data` and `make_submit_transfer_item`.
Remove these methods.

issue_title: Allow all methods accepting `num_results` to take `num_results=None` to mean "all results"
issue_body:
This came up during work on #93 -- we discussed the fact that there's no obvious way to get "all of the results" when you don't know how many there are.

The subtle distinction between "all results" and "no limit" is that some APIs have a service-side limit on the total number of results returned.

So, when a `PaginatedResource` sees `num_results=None`, it should
1. Check if there is a `max_total_results` and set `num_results` to that if found
2. Skip `num_results`-related checking if `num_results is None`

issue_title: non ASCII compatible UTF-8 inputs break on python 2.7
issue_body:
When looking into / writing tests for https://github.com/globus/globus-cli/issues/65, I ran into some `UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode characters` errors from the SDK when using python 2.7

It seems that python 2.7 breaks when it tries to use the default ascii codec to deal with the type inconsistency between string literals being byte strings and `safe_stringify` always returning unicode.

The simplest solution I could think of was to import `unicode_literals` from `__future__` in modules that use `safe_stringify` to make the necessary internal strings be unicode regardless of python version, but was wondering if there was a better solution? I think `safe_stringify` was originally intended to output byte strings for python 2, which might also solve this problem?

issue_title: GitClientBase.get_item_text always returns the file metadata
issue_body:
To retrieve the content of a file, it is mandatory to send the 'text/plain' Accept header, otherwise the file metadata is always returned.

There are two issues:
1. the `VSSClient._send` hard codes `application/json` as Accept header
2. the generated `GitClientBase.get_item_text` must somehow pass `text/plain` as Accept header

issue_title: hex-encode the block number in eth_call
issue_body:
* Version: *?


### What was wrong?

As reported by @chfast :

```
>>> web3.eth.call(tx, 5)
ValueError: {'message': 'invalid argument 1: hex string without 0x prefix', 'code': -32602}
```

To fill in:

- web3 version: _
- web3 provider: _

### How can it be fixed?

Hex encode the block number before making rpc call, probably through the block number formatter in the pythonic middleware: https://github.com/ethereum/web3.py/blob/master/web3/middleware/pythonic.py

Workaround: supply block number already hex-encoded, like: `web3.eth.call(tx, "0x5")`

issue_title: ConciseContract cleanup
issue_body:
### What was wrong?

The `web3.contract.ConciseContract` has some cleanup that could be done to improve the class in small ways.

### How can it be fixed?

1. Update the documentation to make it very clear that `ConciseContract` **only** exposes contract functions and that **all** other `Contract` class methods and properties are not available with the `ConciseContract` API.  This includes `c.address`, `c.abi`, `c.deploy()`, etc.
2. The `_none_addr`  method on the `ConciseContract` class should be moved to a stand-alone function.  This is to remove the potential for collisions between actual methods on the `ConciseContract` and contract ABI function names.
3. In the `__init__` we should check for name collisions between contract methods and class methods.  In the event we find a collision I think we should insert an `@property` method onto the class which raises an `AttributeError` indicating that there was a name collision, the name that collided, and instructions that they should access the function on the primary contract object.

issue_title: Overwritten transactions are not removed
issue_body:
* Version: 0.1.0b15
* Python: 3.5
* OS: all

### What was wrong?

When submitting a transaction that overwrites another transaction, the original transaction is not removed and calls to `getTransaction()` still return the old transaction instead of null.

In geth, the transaction would be removed and returned as `None` from `getTransaction()`.

Example:

```
tester = EthereumTester(auto_mine_transactions=False)

web3 = Web3(EthereumTesterProvider(tester))

original_tx = web3.eth.sendTransaction({
    'from': '...',
    'to': '...',
    'value': 1,
    'gas': 23500,
    'gasPrice': 10,
    'nonce': 0
})

print(web3.eth.getTransaction(original_tx))

replacement_tx = web3.eth.sendTransaction({
    'from': '...',
    'to': '...',
    'value': 2,
    'gas': 23500,
    'gasPrice': 20,
    'nonce': 0
})

print(web3.eth.getTransaction(replacement_tx))
print(web3.eth.getTransaction(original_tx))  # Should be null but still returns transaction
```

### How can it be fixed?

Unsure whether this is a backend problem or not but i imagine there needs to be some tx pool managing functionality added.

issue_title: --file seems to have surprising behavior for upgrade vs install
issue_body:
I have a simplistic view of `conda upgrade` vs `conda install`.

For `upgrade` I assume I don't specify a version number, and Conda will figure it out for me, giving me the latest version of a package that will work with everything else I have installed.

For `install`, if it is a new package, I assume I'll get the latest available version of a package that results in the minimum set of changes to existing packages.

For `install`, if it is an existing package, I assume that I need to provide a specific version number and otherwise Conda will say *"already installed!"*.  If I provide a version number then Conda will do everything in its power to give me that version of that package, and change whatever else it needs to in my Conda environment to make that happen (upgrades and downgrades).

If any of those assumptions are broadly off base then what I have to say next can be ignored.  But if they are reasonable assumptions then the behavior of the `--file` option confuses me.

As per this ML response I just wrote: https://groups.google.com/a/continuum.io/forum/#!msg/anaconda/Q50Yl1V2q9g/yNdqhll4CQAJ

I have now created a version-free list of Conda packages that are found in Anaconda 4.2:

https://gist.github.com/ijstokes/3688030a04639288539aa0e251c526dd#file-anaconda42_pkgs_noversions-txt

One possible way I could imagine using those would be to do:

```bash
for pkg in `cat anaconda42_pkgs_noversions.txt`; do
    conda update -y $pkg
done
```

But since I've got the `--file` option available I can imagine saving myself some bash scripting and just invoke:

```
conda update --file anaconda42_pkgs_noversions.txt
```

But this does not work!  Nothing happens, and I'm told all the packages are already installed.  I **know** they are installed, but I want to `update` them.

Instead it seems I need to do:

```
conda install --file anaconda42_pkgs_noversions.txt
```

This is a surprise!

issue_title: Shebang replacement can exceed length limit
issue_body:
On Linux, there is a hard limit on the length of the shebang path, somewhere between 80 and 127 characters. Currently, conda performs shebang update upon installation, and that can easily exceed that limit in certain setups.

My proposal is to avoid hitting this limit by using the 'env' option for finding the Python interpreter on the path:

``` bash
#!/usr/bin/env python
...
```

I understand that may not be what one needs, but it is better than having a completely broken shebang path when the limit is exceeded.

issue_title: Support package variants
issue_body:
Packages, notably ones containing C extension modules, can be optimized for different hardware (including GPUs).
Allow conda to track multiple variants of such packages, such that `conda install` could then fetch and install the appropriate variant, depending on a) user requests, or b) based on the specific hardware available.
As a point in case, The TensorFlow package now emits warnings of the form
```
W tensorflow/core/platform/cpu_feature_guard.cc:45] The TensorFlow library wasn't compiled to use SSE4.1 instructions, but these are available on your machine and could speed up CPU computations.
```
letting users wonder how to obtain a variant that has SSE4.1 support, etc.

issue_title: `conda install -f` implies `--no-deps` which is a problem for meta-packages
issue_body:
I believe this is related to #466 and #1255, but perhaps not.

First, it is unclear to me _why_ the `-f` option to `conda install` should imply `--no-deps`.  Certainly I would say that most people would not expect this to be the case.

But that issue aside, it is a problem for meta-packages which _only_ consist of dependencies.  If I do `conda install -f jupyter` then I want to force-install all the dependency packages specified the jupyter meta-package.  More than "I want", in fact "I expect" that is what will happen.  But it doesn't.  And I would suggest that the average user is not going to pick up on the visual hints that force-install of meta-packages do not install the dependencies.  They will just be confused that it hasn't happened or oblivious that it hasn't happened.

issue_title: sqlite3 is built without readline support
issue_body:
Helping out at a Software Carpentry bootcamp last week, several attendees encountered problems with up-arrow not working at the sqlite3 prompt.

In at least one case, this appears to have been due to the conda provided sqlite3 being built without readline support.

(Filing this against the base conda project as sqlite3 doesn't appear to be part of conda-recipes)

issue_title: Local Conda installation failing due to permissions on shared hosting
issue_body:
I can't seem to get any help with this on  [stackoverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/45018272/local-conda-installation-failing-due-to-permissions-on-shared-hosting) or the [google group](https://groups.google.com/a/continuum.io/forum/#!topic/conda/IJg6etdzM3c).

I had thought I could create an isolated, local installation. But it turns out you seem to need links to system packages belonging to root. I think this is the essence of [this question](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/41640056/do-conda-environments-have-access-to-root-environment-system-packages), which remains unanswered.

I've been using conda quite a bit with non-root installs on servers. Right now, I'm trying to install it on a Bluehost shared server. They have recently changed the ssh shell to a jailshell that does not allow linking to system packages. I did not realize this would be an obstacle. However...

    [/home7/arielbal/miniconda3] >>> /home7/arielbal/test/conda
    PREFIX=/home7/arielbal/test/conda
    installing: python-3.6.1-2 ...
    tar: bin/2to3: Cannot create symlink to `2to3-3.6': Permission denied
    tar: bin/idle3: Cannot create symlink to `idle3.6': Permission denied
    tar: bin/pydoc: Cannot create symlink to `pydoc3.6': Permission denied
    tar: bin/pydoc3: Cannot create symlink to `pydoc3.6': Permission denied
    tar: bin/python: Cannot create symlink to `python3.6': Permission denied
    tar: bin/python3: Cannot create symlink to `python3.6': Permission denied
    tar: bin/python3-config: Cannot create symlink to `python3.6m-config': Permission denied
    tar: bin/python3.6-config: Cannot create symlink to `python3.6m-config': Permission denied
    tar: bin/pyvenv: Cannot create symlink to `pyvenv-3.6': Permission denied
    tar: lib/libpython3.6m.so: Cannot create symlink to `libpython3.6m.so.1.0': Permission denied
    tar: share/man/man1/python3.1: Cannot create symlink to `python3.6.1': Permission denied
    tar: Exiting with failure status due to previous errors

Bluehost does allow compiling (unlike greengeeks.com), so I actually went through the trouble of compiling the dependencies to get the initial miniconda install. I was eventually able to get that to work. But when I tried to install further packages, I again ran into permissions/linking issues.

Is there a way to create a local install conda in a completely portable way that will handle all dependencies not currently linkable on the system?

gr2ogr --version
ogr2ogr: error while loading shared libraries: libicui18n.so.56: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory

I noticed that the icu package is this version:

I've been wrestling with this issue for the past day. Can someone help?

issue_title: Windows activate does not work in a batch file
issue_body:
See https://groups.google.com/a/continuum.io/forum/#!topic/anaconda/QMwqUbaVQDc. If you run 

```bat
activate py33
python -V
```

in a batch file, it does not work, but if you run

```bat
activate py33 && python -V
```

it does. I did not verify it yet.

issue_title: conda skeleton can't see an old version of cherrypy
issue_body:
When I try to make a skeleton of CherryPy version 3.7.0, conda denies that it exists in PyPi:
```
azuolas:conda_temp pmlandwehr$ conda skeleton pypi cherrypy --version 3.7.0
Error: Version 3.7.0 of cherrypy is not available on PyPI.
azuolas:conda_temp pmlandwehr$ 
```

However, [there *is* a page for it in PyPi,](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/CherryPy/3.7.0) with source. What's going on?

issue_title: pip can silently downgrade conda installed packages
issue_body:

issue_title: Show who is assigned in the case overview
ctable.fromdataframe doesn't preserve datetime indices.
issue_body:
Let's consider following case:

```python
date_range = pd.date_range(start='2017-01-01', end='2017-01-03', freq='D')
cols = ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E']
df = pd.DataFrame(index=date_range, data=np.random.rand(len(date_range), len(cols)), columns=cols)
df
```
| A | B | C | D | E |
| - | - | - | - | - |
| 2017-01-01 | 0.140845 | 0.619423 | 0.697564 | 0.975552 | 0.419815 |
| 2017-01-02 | 0.327959 | 0.657860 | 0.234037 | 0.705020 | 0.656515 |
| 2017-01-03 | 0.065697 | 0.543179 | 0.517165 | 0.692339 | 0.340754 |

```python
df_table = bcolz.ctable.fromdataframe(df)
df_table
```

```
ctable((3,), [('A', '<f8'), ('B', '<f8'), ('C', '<f8'), ('D', '<f8'), ('E', '<f8')])
  nbytes: 120; cbytes: 80.00 KB; ratio: 0.00
  cparams := cparams(clevel=5, shuffle=1, cname='blosclz', quantize=0)
[( 0.14084531,  0.61942324,  0.69756392,  0.9755517,  0.41981526)
 ( 0.3279591,  0.65785982,  0.23403727,  0.70502042,  0.65651498)
 ( 0.06569704,  0.54317941,  0.51716506,  0.69233858,  0.34075387)]
```

and if we try to retrieve dataframe from table we get:

```python
df_ = df_table.todataframe()
df_
```

| A | B | C | D | E |
| - | - | - | - | - |
| 0 | 0.140845 | 0.619423 | 0.697564 | 0.975552 | 0.419815 |
| 1 | 0.327959 | 0.657860 | 0.234037 | 0.705020 | 0.656515 |
| 2 | 0.065697 | 0.543179 | 0.517165 | 0.692339 | 0.340754 |

A possible workaround is to duplicate index column to regular one:
```python
df['_index'] = df.index
df_table = bcolz.ctable.fromdataframe(df)
df_table.attrs['_index'] = '_index'
```

In this case, Bcolz preserves datetime type. And after retrieving you can assign your index back.

issue_title: Not getting disconnect events
issue_body:
Hi, this is a great looking Websocket client implementation!  I'd love to use it but I need to iron out a few details. Perhaps I'm misunderstanding something, but I am not getting disconnect events when I intentionally disconnect my laptop in order to test Lomond's event functionality.   I've tried using threading explicitly, via gevent monkey patching, and explicitly using greenlets for the event loop.  I've tried both the `connect()` method and `persist()`.  Here is the test script I'm running:
```
# import gevent.monkey
# gevent.monkey.patch_all()

import lomond
import lomond.persist
import datetime
import threading

log = logging


class WebsocketClient:
    def __init__(self, url):
        self.socket = lomond.websocket.WebSocket(url)

    def run(self):
        def _event_loop():
            print("socket client {} OPENED".format(self.socket))

            #for event in lomond.persist.persist(self.socket):
            for event in self.socket:
                self.on_event(event)

            print("socket client {} CLOSED".format(self.socket))

        # gevent.spawn(_event_loop)
        # gevent.sleep(0)
        threading.Thread(target=_event_loop).start()

    def stop(self):
        self.socket.close()

    def on_event(self, event):
        print("{} event: {} ".format(self.socket, event))
        print("")


client = WebsocketClient("ws://echo.websocket.org")
client.run()

gevent.sleep(5)

try:
    while True:
        client.socket.send_text(
            unicode("hello {}".format(datetime.datetime.now()))
        )
        gevent.sleep(10)
except:
    client.stop()
```

I'm running Python 2.7,  and I have tried with 3.6 as well.  What happens is that if I disconnect from the LAN, I continue to receive poll events. Instead of getting a disconnect event, it just keeps going.  The loop that periodically sends messages doesn't raise an Exception either.

issue_title: [Enhancement] Update the binaries for Windows to GnuCOBOL 1.1
issue_body:
An "official" binary package with MinGW can be downloaded from http://sourceforge.net/projects/open-cobol/files/gnu-cobol/1.1/GnuCOBOL_1.1_MinGW_BDB_PDcurses_MPIR.7z

For a quite complete list of the differences between OC 1.1 and GC 1.1 see http://opencobol.add1tocobol.com/gnucobol/#what-are-the-differences-between-opencobol-1-1-and-gnucobol-1-1
There are some additional fixes, compare BUGS.txt in https://github.com/OpenCobolIDE/OpenCobol-Win32-MinGW/pull/1 to the new version included in the distribution.

This official MinGW package was build using the latests versions of prerequisites available:

    MinGW (minimal packages)
    GCC 4.8.1
    BDB 6.1.23
    PDcurses 3.4
    MPIR 2.7.0 (gmpcompat, with all processor optimization removed)

It doesn't contain "anything more" (not even the marvelous Programmer's Guide as it's not part of the GnuCOBOL project yet) and uses MPIR instead of GMP and is digitally signed (both the package and the GnuCOBOL parts).

If you need an unpacked version at git you're free to mirror it at OpenCobol-Win32-MinGW:master, too, but it seems a good idea to rename this project.

Please drop a note at https://sourceforge.net/p/open-cobol/discussion/contrib/thread/5d035f17/ when you have a release which includes this package, thank you.

BTW: What do you think about an installer that doesn't contain any GnuCOBOL binaries but adds an option to select a package for installation (MinGW, maybe MinGW64, Visual Studio 8/9/10/11/12/14 [either with both and 32/64 or with the option to choose] which would be downloaded from SourceForge [maybe as a fallback from gnu.org] and maybe all packages two times, one with BDB, one with VBISAM) - with a default to MinGW?

issue_title: Filter by count excludes despite exclude=False when at least one index does not match the pattern
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm desperately trying to explicitly select the last index of a sequence (`index-000001`, `index-000002`, ...), and the best filter for that is `count`. However, the 'inclusion' behavior that I need here does not work.

After digging into the code, I realized the following line in the `filter_by_count` method, which is executed when at least one index does not match the provided pattern (which is almost always the case), overwrites the parameter `exclude` and the 'include' behavior is lost.

https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/5fdcc337625c6a7e9aeeb90de625b54b92cbbcb7/curator/indexlist.py#L943

Do you see any workaround I could use for my situation? I'd like to avoid selecting by age, which is kind of uncertain depending on when the action will be executed.

issue_title: Set default timeout_override for delete_snapshots action
issue_body:
When using the `delete_snapshots` action, the default timeout of 30s is not sufficient. Actions which have to wait for the on an HTTP request for the Elasticsearch cluster to complete the action generally have a long default timeout, `delete_snapshots` is an exception to that.

### Expected Behavior
The default timeout for the `delete_snapshots` action should be sufficient for most common situations.

### Actual Behavior
Without setting a `timeout_override` option, the `delete_snapshots` action frequently fails. Relevant log entries for this failure:
```
2018-01-18 15:57:15,962 INFO      Preparing Action ID: 400, "delete_snapshots"
2018-01-18 15:57:15,967 INFO      Trying Action ID: 400, "delete_snapshots": Delete snapshots of active states of now read-only indices
2018-01-18 15:57:46,005 ERROR     Failed to complete action: delete_snapshots.  <class 'curator.exceptions.FailedExecution'>: Unable to get snapshot information from repository: gcs_backup_active.  Error: ConnectionTimeout caused by - ReadTimeoutError(HTTPConnectionPool(host=u'elasticsearch', port=9200): Read timed out. (read timeout=30))
```

### Steps to Reproduce the Problem
I don't know how reliable these steps will be, but I'll describe my situation.
  1. Run Elasticsearch in the Google Kubernetes Engine
  1. Use the `repository-gcs` plugin to store shapshots to google cloud storage.
  1. Index a moderate amount of log data, we're collecting about 150 events/second totalling about 6GB per day, using daily indices.
  1. Take frequent snapshots of these indices.
  1. After a significant number of snapshots have accumulated, configure Curator to delete old snapshots. My configuration is:
```yaml
      400:
        action: delete_snapshots
        description: Delete snapshots of active states of now read-only indices
        options:
          disable_action: False
          repository: gcs_backup_active
          ignore_empty_list: True
        filters:
        - filtertype: age
          source: creation_date
          direction: older
          unit: days
          unit_count: 3
```
  1. These `delete_snapshot` actions will fail with the log entries shown above.
  1. Add `timeout_override` to the options of this job extending the timeout enough to allow the actions to pass.

Relevant log entries from a successful execution with increased timeout:
```
2018-01-18 16:02:43,311 INFO      Preparing Action ID: 400, "delete_snapshots"
2018-01-18 16:02:43,315 INFO      Trying Action ID: 400, "delete_snapshots": Delete snapshots of active states of now read-only indices
2018-01-18 16:04:16,166 INFO      Deleting selected snapshots
2018-01-18 16:05:28,218 INFO      Deleting snapshot ssa_201801120407...
2018-01-18 16:06:24,931 INFO      Deleting snapshot ssa_201801120411...
2018-01-18 16:07:55,201 INFO      Deleting snapshot ssa_201801120455...
2018-01-18 16:08:42,957 INFO      Deleting snapshot ssa_201801120459...
2018-01-18 16:09:34,815 INFO      Deleting snapshot ssa_201801120503...
```

### Specifications

- Version:
  - Curator version 5.4.1
  - Elasticsearch version 5.5.1
- Platform: Google Kubernetes Engine (linux, docker)
- Storage Plugin: `repository-gcs`

issue_title: Increase _MAX_BLOB_CHUNK_SIZE_BYTES?
issue_body: Hi @alfpark I'm back trying again to upload some >200GB blobs since the [size limitation was greatly increased late last year](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/general-availability-larger-block-blobs-in-azure-storage/). As I understand that article, the maximum chunk/block size is increased from 4MB to 100MB. In blobxfer.py we still seem to have hardcoded `_MAX_BLOB_CHUNK_SIZE_BYTES = 4194304`, and the 50,000 chunk limit, so it won't initiate an upload above the old 195GB limit. Should that constant be increased?

issue_title: [DocumentDB] Listing connection strings doesn't support table output by default
issue_body:
Running the following command results in a blank line being printed:

```shell
az documentdb list-connection-strings -n <NAME> -g <GROUP>
```

You can obviously use a custom output type, but it would be ideal if this command printed a table of connection strings by default.

issue_title: Telemetry between Server-side and Client-side is broken for Interactive Mode
issue_body:
### Description

Interactive mode uses the same client-request-id for all commands, which breaks the information sent out

issue_title: AzureStack: Support for registering cloud just with ARM endpoint
issue_body:
This will immensely reduce the number of parameters needed for cloud register from 8 to just 2 (ArmEndpoint and Name)

1. Call Arm Metdata Endpoint (like https://management.azure.com/metadata/endpoints?api-version=1.0) and populate the other fields from the metadata needed for the cloud register

Arm metadata provides the following details
```
{"galleryEndpoint":"https://gallery.azure.com","graphEndpoint":"https://graph.windows.net/","portalEndpoint":"https://portal.azure.com","authentication":{"loginEndpoint":"https://login.windows.net","audiences":["https://management.core.windows.net/","https://management.azure.com/"]}}
```

2. During az login, also call /providers (just like /subscriptions) and populate the storage endpoint suffix and keyvault endpoint suffix

issue_title: [MySQL/Postgres] Can't list servers across a subscription
issue_body:
### Description

The `az mysql server list` (and `az postgres server list`) commands currently requires specifying a resource group, which isn't consistent with most of the other `list` commands in the CLI. It would be great if these commands allowed omitting the group, and simply specifying it when you want to filter the list to a specific group.

---

### Environment summary

**Install Method:** How did you install the CLI? (e.g. pip, interactive script, apt-get, Docker, MSI, nightly)  
Answer here: nightly

**CLI Version:** What version of the CLI and modules are installed? (Use `az --version`)  
Answer here: nightly

**OS Version:** What OS and version are you using?  
Answer here: macOS

**Shell Type:** What shell are you using? (e.g. bash, cmd.exe, Bash on Windows)  
Answer here: bash

  Size: 5874      	Blocks: 16         IO Block: 4096   regular file
Device: 2dh/45d	Inode: 4429        Links: 1

Modify: 2016-08-01 18:29:52.128670025 +0000

```

Should be 0600. Please do not repeat the same mistakes azure-xplat-cli did.

issue_title: SDN Multiple Delete operations with unconsistent response
issue_body:

  **az network lb frontend-ip delete 
     az network lb probe delete
     az network lb address-pool delete 
     az network lb rule delete
     az network lb inbound-nat-rule delete
     az network lb inbound-nat-pool delete

issue_title: Multiple NICs applied to Windows server inconsistently
issue_body:
### Description
Azure CLI 2.0 applies NICs inconsistently

Steps to reproduce:

Create two NICs: NIC1. NIC2
Create a windows server 2012 R2 VM with the two NICs
Wait for Windows VM to launch and then log in to the machine and check the interfaces
Results:
Sometimes NIC1 = ethernet1 and NIC2=ethernet2 on the OS and sometimes NIC1 = ethernet2 and NIC2=ethernet1. This did not reproduce when using CLI v1, the first NIC applied was always ethernet1. Is there any way to get this consistent?

---

### Environment summary

**Install Method:** How did you install the CLI? (e.g. pip, interactive script, apt-get, Docker, MSI, nightly)  
Answer here: pip

**CLI Version:** What version of the CLI and modules are installed? (Use `az --version`)  
Answer here: 2.0.1

**OS Version:** What OS and version are you using?  
Answer here: Win7 64

**Shell Type:** What shell are you using? (e.g. bash, cmd.exe, Bash on Windows)  
Answer here: cmd

issue_title: Extensions should be able to register cloud-specific information
issue_body: Extensions need a way to register any custom endpoints for different clouds. Core CLI modules manually add their entries to the core profile code, but this isn't available to extensions, so there must be some way (perhaps through the extension metadata) that this information can be applied.

issue_title: vm: support write accelerators
issue_body:
This is for a new CRP service feature, requiring M series (64+ cores)
3 commands to update
1. `vm create --enable-write-accelerator`
2. `vm update --write-accelerator`: to enable and disable this option.
3. `vm disk attach --enable-write-accelerator` 

For the same support in vmss, @gatneil, what is your opinions on this? The change is simple, but I doubt we would have scale-sets using this. With 2 instances by default, that will take away 128 core which is already over the default quota for most subscriptions. If we don't know, maybe we can wait till we have users who do need this?

issue_title: [Monitor] metrics list-defintions: bad error message
issue_body:
when I run " az monitor metrics list-definitions --resource explorer-1"
I get a message that says "usage error: --resource_uri ID | --resource_uri NAME --resource-group NAME --resource_uri-type TYPE [--resource_uri-parent PARENT] [--resource_uri-namespace NAMESPACE]"

issue_title: No module named azure in pyvenv
issue_body:
### Description

Installing azure-cli to a Python 3 venv produces invalid `az` command.

```
$ python3.6 -m venv ~/azure
$ ~/azure/bin/pip install -q azure-cli
$ ~/azure/bin/python --version        
Python 3.6.1
$ ~/azure/bin/az
/usr/local/opt/python/bin/python2.7: No module named azure

Whatever magic the az script is attempting to do is apparently not detecting the virtual environment and is instead invoking an old Python from a different environment where azure is not installed.

issue_title: Single-version API clients are potentially not forward-compatible
issue_body:
I keep forgetting to ask about this: single-version API clients are not being generated with the same patterns as multi-version APIs. The most notable omissions are the `api_version` and `profile` args on the main client `__init__`; this prevents the current API version from being "pinned" by a caller, so a future SDK upgrade that adds a new (potentially incompatible) version could break it. They're also missing the `models` static property on the main client class, and the `api_version` property on the operations classes. These omissions prevent the use of the same dynamic patterns used on a multi-version client, and can potentially introduce breaking changes when a new API version is eventually introduced to that resource type, as the caller is required to "float" its version to the latest. Since the SDK objects for single-version clients aren't generated with versioned namespaces, it's not even possible to pin them statically...

eg, ComputeManagementClient has multiple versions, where ContainerInstanceManagementClient only has one; note the significant differences required in the way they'd be used in a version-pinned fashion.

Was this an oversight, or is there a reason not to follow the multi-version pattern on all clients/models?

issue_title: Subscription ID is not removed from request or some response bodies
issue_body:
Not, I think, for the same reason as #16, but it might make sense to fix both simultaneously.

This issue is actually two related problems with slightly different solutions:
* The subscription ID isn't removed from the request body because `process_request` only replaces the `uri` field.
* It's not removed from the response body because `_replace_subscription_id`'s regex substitution is case sensitive: it searches for `/subscriptions/([^/]+/` but sometimes it appears in the URI as `/Subscriptions/...`.

issue_title: Crash with aiohttp 3.1.x related to use of StreamReader
issue_body:
I'm getting a crash using aiobotocore 0.6.0 with aiohttp 3.1.0 (released yesterday 2018-03-21). It appears to be related to botocore's response checking:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 20, in <module>
    loop.run_until_complete(main())
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/3.6.4_4/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/asyncio/base_events.py", line 467, in run_until_complete

  File "test.py", line 12, in main
    response = await client.describe_table(TableName='orbit-prod-identities')
  File "/Users/adam/Desktop/aiobotocore-test/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiobotocore/client.py", line 94, in _make_api_call
    operation_model, request_dict)
  File "/Users/adam/Desktop/aiobotocore-test/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiobotocore/endpoint.py", line 290, in _send_request
    exception)):
  File "/Users/adam/Desktop/aiobotocore-test/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiobotocore/endpoint.py", line 322, in _needs_retry
    caught_exception=caught_exception, request_dict=request_dict)
  File "/Users/adam/Desktop/aiobotocore-test/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/botocore/hooks.py", line 227, in emit
    return self._emit(event_name, kwargs)
  File "/Users/adam/Desktop/aiobotocore-test/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/botocore/hooks.py", line 210, in _emit
    response = handler(**kwargs)
  File "/Users/adam/Desktop/aiobotocore-test/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/botocore/retryhandler.py", line 183, in __call__
    if self._checker(attempts, response, caught_exception):
  File "/Users/adam/Desktop/aiobotocore-test/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/botocore/retryhandler.py", line 251, in __call__
    caught_exception)

TypeError: a bytes-like object is required, not 'StreamReader'

# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
import asyncio

import aiobotocore


async def main():
    session = aiobotocore.get_session()

if __name__ == '__main__':

    loop.run_until_complete(main())
```

issue_title: How to statically compile example
issue_body:
I successfully got a working example to work from [your wiki](https://cmusphinx.github.io/wiki/tutorialpocketsphinx/#full-code-listing).

However, I am trying to package this as a statically linked executable.

Running,
```
gcc -o speechtotext speechtotext.c -DMODELDIR=\"`pkg-config --variable=modeldir pocketsphinx`\" `pkg-config --cflags --libs pocketsphinx sphinxbase`
```

produces a dynamically linked executable just fine. I am attempting to create the statically linked executable with the command:
```
gcc -static -o speechtotext speechtotext.c -DMODELDIR=\"`pkg-config --variable=modeldir pocketsphinx`\" `pkg-config --cflags --libs pocketsphinx sphinxbase`
```

However, I am getting the error:
```
/usr/bin/ld: cannot find -lpulse
/usr/bin/ld: cannot find -lpulse-simple
```
Checking the result of the subcommand `pkg-config --cflags --libs pocketsphinx sphinxbase`, I see the result `-I/usr/local/include -I/usr/local/include/sphinxbase -I/usr/local/include/pocketsphinx -I/usr/local/include -I/usr/local/include/sphinxbase -L/usr/local/lib -lpocketsphinx -lsphinxbase -lsphinxad -lpulse -lpulse-simple -lpthread -lm`.

I notice that by default, the debian installtion of `libpulse-dev` installs the libraries `libpulse.so` and `libpulse-simple.so` to `/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/`. Hence I tried to symlink these into `/usr/local/lib` where the `pkg-config` command looks for the `-lpocketsphinx -lsphinxbase -lsphinxad` etc.

However, I get the exact same error. Any idea why this is happening? Would this be something wrong with the way I am trying to statically link? Or is an executable relying on `libpulse` fundamentally unable to statically compile for some reason? I got the information about the exact package listing for where libpulse installs for Debian based systems [here](https://packages.ubuntu.com/trusty/amd64/libpulse-dev/filelist).

Any help would be much appreciated

issue_title: Download as PDF or Tex does not work with images created inline using %matplotlib notebook
issue_body:
Jupyter 5.2.2

If you create images (graphs etc) using the %matplotlib notebook magic then images created inline are not rendered in Tex or PDF output.

Instead the images is replaced with 
<IPython.core.display.Javascript object>
<IPython.core.display.HTML object>

The suggested workround of replacing %matplotlib notebook with %matplotlib inline before using 'Download as' is not convienient as it does not work in all cases. 
For example (given below) the figure.set_size_inches methods produces different results.

Example code to reproduce the issue
```
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

#%matplotlib inline
%matplotlib notebook

fig = plt.figure()
fig.set_size_inches(9.5,6, forward=True)
plt.plot(np.arange(0,10,0.1))
plt.plot(np.arange(10,0,-0.1))
```
File -> Download As -> PDF (Via LaTeX)

This issue is discussed here:
[https://stackoverflow.com/questions/35305607/jupyter-notebook-matplotlib-figures-missing-in-exported-pdf](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/35305607/jupyter-notebook-matplotlib-figures-missing-in-exported-pdf)

issue_title: [Feature Request] Live NBViewer
issue_body:
## Issue
In situations like tutorials, tutorial leaders often provide jupyter notebooks to be followed along with while also using a master notebook themselves. Oftentimes these master notebooks can be subjected to changes and the changes are not easily legible in the tutorial environment. A tutorial participant being able to view that notebook live on their machine from a website would be of huge use to those struggling to keep up with changes to a master notebook. 

## Proposal
I'm not entirely sure how it would work. I have talked to someone who has had this issue and wrote a shell script to continuously `git add` `git commit` the master notebook he is working on, while users can keep refreshing the rendering of the github hosting of the notebook. Maybe there are some technical limitations, but wrapping the entire rendering of the master notebook in an iframe would prevent viewers of the master notebook from using it (I think?) This master notebook would have to be hosted somewhere, and be able to communicate with the instance of the master notebook being live edited by a tutorial teacher. This is really beyond my skill level.

Anyways, this feature would be really nice, I was encouraged by some Cal Poly students to post an issue and I hope it makes sense!

issue_title: Artifact in LaTeX/MathJax rendering
issue_body:
I'm getting the following artifact of a right sided border on rendered MathJax objects (see image) from either manually created LaTeX code (as below) or Sympy expression renderings. The issue happens for both $ ... $ and $$ ... $$ items, where the MathJax object terminates; inline expressions will have them, too. This was working on the default install of a plain Python installation, using notebook 4.1, but under the most recent distribution of Anaconda, it's happening.

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/197778/12616595/0625ccb8-c4da-11e5-9c58-3f1e90dabf36.png)

```
We know the following:
* Side AC = 500
* Side BC = 300
* Angle BAC = 36.87
* Angle ABC = 90

We need to solve for side AB. As described, angle BAC allows us to use its cosine ratio to derive AB, since it is adjacent and side AC is opposite angle ABC, and thus is the hypotenuse:

$\cos{36.87} = \frac{AB}{500}$  
$500 \cdot \cos{36.87} = AB$  
$400 = AB$

We can check this using the Pythagorean theorem, $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$:  
$300^2+b^2=500^2$  
$90000+b^2=250000$  
$b^2 = 160000$  
$b = \sqrt{160000} = 400$
```

I can do further testing, but wanted to at least bring this to your attention first to know if I'm missing something here. Thanks!

issue_title: Feature request: Include namespaces in result when matching multiple rule sources
issue_body:
```python
import yara
rules = yara.compile(sources={
    'namespace1':'rule dummy { condition: true }',
    'namespace2':'rule dummy { condition: true }',
    'namespace3':'rule dummy2 { condition: true }'}
)
rules.match(data='hello world')  # Returns [dummy, dummy2, dummy]
```

The whole point of the namespaces is to distinguish between the different rules when they may have the same name. Please include the namespaces in the match result, e.g.
```
[namespace1:dummy, namespace2:dummy, namespace3:dummy2]
```
or similar. As it stands, I may have to implement a callback to keep track of which namespaces and rules were matched.

## Environment
I'm running `yara-python v3.5.0`, installed via `pip` on Mac OS X.

issue_title: Redesign the conversion/validation exceptions
issue_body:
### Unify aggregate exception classes

The current separation between `ModelConversionError` and `ModelValidationError` provides no informational value at all, since the error class is picked solely based on which model-level routine was invoked and has nothing to do with what actually went wrong.

To illustrate, the *exact same* conversion error may appear as a `ModelConversionError` or `ModelValidationError`, depending on if you called `Model.convert()`, or `Model.validate()`, respectively. This is unnecessary complexity and a potential source of confusion (#137).

Also, since these classes may represent data on *several* errors, each of which may independently be either a conversion error or a validation error, it's clear that communicating anything about the error types through the name of the class is just not viable.

Based on this, I think the aggregate exception classes should be unified.

### Improve exception message format

Right now the aggregate error message structure only contains the names of the offending fields coupled with textual messages.

The format should be redesigned to accommodate at least the following items for each individual error:

  * name of the field or index of the item (#307), whichever is applicable
  * type of the error (conversion/validation)
  * human-readable textual message
  * structured response data from the validator/converter function (e.g., error code).

issue_title: Manifest file for including or skipping files to upload
issue_body:
Feature request received:

> I’m trying to setup a tool that will allow me to archive a project to the DDS from a cinderblock storage.   There is a lot of stuff we don’t need to keep in the archive, and so I’m working on how to maintain our current project structure while being able to upload just the components we want.   I was wondering if it would be possible to have the ddsclient read a file of directories (either for inclusion in the upload, or for exclusion) instead of only being able to provide the folders in the command line. It sounds similar to a .gitignore file. I could see reading a .ddsignore (or .ddskeep) file, but having this with the  `--include` and `--exclude` options on download would be unclear. We could build on this pattern with `--include-file` and `--exclude-file`, and also apply it to upload

issue_title: Issue w/ gradient of function of gradient
issue_body:
Hi,

I've noticed that autograd is giving me a different-than-expected result when I try to take the gradient (wrt one set of parameters) of the square of the norm of the gradient (wrt a different set of parameters). For example, when I attempt to use the following code to compute d/dA [ || d/dx x^T A x ||^2 ], with A symmetric,

```python
def quad_score(x, A):
    """
    computes quadratic form: x^T A x
    """
    return np.dot(x, np.dot(A, x))

def qs_grad_norm_sq(A, x):
    """
    computes ||d/dx [x^T A x]||^2
    """
    grad_quad_score = grad(quad_score)
    grad_x = grad_quad_score(x, A)
    return np.dot(grad_x, grad_x)

grad_qs_grad_norm_sq = grad(qs_grad_norm_sq)
```

It's worth noting that the gradient wrt A returned by autograd _does_ agree with the finite differences obtained when using qs_grad_norm_sq() as above, but qs_grad_norm_sq() seems to be returning values that are very slightly off. Any suggestions? 

Thanks!

issue_title: coalesce errors while there are running jobs
issue_body:
This is close to working, though I need to rework the way exceptions get aggregated according to @olson-sean-k's suggestion: https://github.com/buildinspace/peru/tree/coalesce

There are two conflicting scenarios that we need to get right:

- When I have long running jobs, I don't want to wait until the very end to find out that there was an error. It would be nice to get some sort of feedback immediately.
- When there's nothing else to wait for, and peru is just going to print my error and exit, I don't want it to get printed twice.

Here's what we want to do:

- Gather all caught exceptions at the fork point, and reraise a container exception type that includes all of them in a list. Tests looking for a specific exception type will need to traverse that list, but will otherwise still work. It probably makes sense for nested gathers to flatten each other. For example, if there's an error inside a recursive module, we want to avoid something like `GatherError(GatherError(RealError))` by unpacking the inner gather.
- Have gather be a `PrintableError` that displays all of its contained error messages, and rely on the existing error printing in `main`. It would be nice if it was aware of verbosity somehow, for stack traces.
- Use the fancy display to show error information while jobs are still running. This will probably involve holding onto display handles after releasing job locks somehow. Maybe even add support for red text in the display...

issue_title: Move wallet keys outside of the "kernel" and into "CLI"
issue_body:
We have three types of access credentials: 

a) Name ownership keys
b) Data signing keys
a) Access keys for storage backends 

The diagram below shows which components within Core that have access to them: 

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/669932/24163113/fb1fdd8a-0e3f-11e7-846d-21f33178da4e.png)

The components of Core are: 

1) CLI (this is a client but is being developed as part of the Core repo)
2) Kernel (this sometimes gets referred to as "Core", this process has most of the complexity) 
3) Indexer (for processing the underlying blockchain) 

Core needs to have access to "access keys for storage backends" but name ownership keys and data signing keys will only be with clients (i.e., the CLI or any other JavaScript client) in the future.

Current status is that we have moved "data keys" to client-only but name ownership keys can be accessed by the kernel process.

issue_title: Bad HResult error codes with IOException on Linux
Update _get_ranked_matches comment to describe ordering by SSL too
issue_body:
(Slightly related to #2059)

One technique for managing Nginx vhosts is to have two config files per server name, one for HTTP and one for HTTPS. Both files would contain the same server_name, and the Nginx configurator would pick one arbitrarily to install the certificate in.

Instead, configurator.py's `_get_ranked_matches` should use the SSL status of a vhost as part of its ranking, and preferentially install certificates in a vhost which already has SSL enabled.

issue_title: Pass Entity into Message Request
issue_body:
Hello, i am using Pyhton 3.5.2 and the latest version 1.4.0 of this python-sdk.

For a use case, the user should choose a contact name from a contact list (which is stored as an entity in IBM Watson Assistant). 
So, possibly, there are multiple contact names matching the user input e.g. when only the firstname is given by the user. For example "michael" gives back 4 suitable contact names. Therefore, i present the 4 names (with fullname)  and the user should choose one of them.

Therefore i want to use the possibility to pass the already filtered contact entity values (4 contacts) into the second bot request, as stated in the documentation https://www.ibm.com/watson/developercloud/assistant/api/v1/python.html?python#message. Beside the `context`, it is also possible to pass the `entities`:

> Entities to use when evaluating the message. Include entities from the previous response to continue using those entities rather than detecting entities in the new input.

I did that with this python example:

```python
# IBM API Text Request
response = ibm_conversation.message(
   workspace_id=ibm_workspace,
   input={'text': message.lower()},
   context=response["context"],  # context from the previous request
   entities=response["context"]["entities"]  #recognized entities from the previous request
)
```

However, the result of the second request is not as expected. There are still 4 entity values detected (the same that i passed into the message request) and all of them were matched to the second user input text which is not correct.

Example:
First user input:
> "i want to call michael"

First answer:
> Okay, there are 4 contact matching: "Michael W, Michael Z, Michael Y, Michael Z. Please choose one!".

Second user input:
> "Okay, i choose Z".

Second bot answer:
> Okay, there are 4 contact matching: "Michael W, Michael Z, Michael Y, Michael Z. Please choose one!"

It is because in the response, i see that the string "Z" is extracted as the four entities.

For me it would be logical to only get the "Michael Z" as extracted entity for the user input message "Okay, i choose Z".

The main goal of this, is that there are also other contact names with the lastname "Z", so when i do not pass the already filtered entity from the first bot response into the second bot request, it gives me back all contacts matching "Z" and this is not what i want.

Any suggestions?
Thanks!
Josef

issue_title: PATCH: Check delay_before_tx/rx for None in serialposix.py
issue_body:
Hello,

Here's a small fix for an issue with the default settings for RS485Settings. In rs485.py delay_before_tx is set to None by default. But in serialposix.py it is unconditionally multiplied by 1000 which fails. I think it's better to leave buf[1] at 0 in that case. The same for delay_before_rx.

Maarten

```patch
--- serialposix.py	Wed Jun 08 12:27:07 2016
+++ serialposix_new.py	Wed Jun 08 12:33:17 2016
@@ -139,8 +139,10 @@
                         buf[0] |= SER_RS485_RTS_AFTER_SEND
                     else:
                         buf[0] &= ~SER_RS485_RTS_AFTER_SEND
-                    buf[1] = int(rs485_settings.delay_before_tx * 1000)
-                    buf[2] = int(rs485_settings.delay_before_rx * 1000)
+                    if rs485_settings.delay_before_tx is not None:
+                        buf[1] = int(rs485_settings.delay_before_tx * 1000)
+                    if rs485_settings.delay_before_rx is not None:
+                        buf[2] = int(rs485_settings.delay_before_rx * 1000)
                 else:
                     buf[0] = 0  # clear SER_RS485_ENABLED
                 fcntl.ioctl(self.fd, TIOCSRS485, buf)
```

issue_title: Column names containing commas should be wrapped in quotes when saving to CSV
issue_body:
Observed Behavior:

SFrame.save("foo.csv") knows that string values containing commas should be wrapped in quotes when saved in csv (comma-delimited). But it does not do that for column names (headers).

```python
import turicreate as tc
print tc.__version__  # => 5.0b2

tc.SFrame({"look,comma": [  # column name with a comma
    "wow, amazing",         # string with comma will get wrapped in quotes
    "wonderful"             # ordinary string, no wrap
]}).save("foo.csv")
```

```bash
$ cat foo.csv   # file won't load using SFrame.read_csv
look,comma      # column name did not get wrapped
"wow, amazing"  # column value got wrapped
wonderful
```

Expected Behavior:

Just like column values, column named should be wrapped with quotes if necessary.
This is consistent with other software packages, for instance in pandas:

```python
import pandas as pd
print pd.__version__  # => 0.19.1

pd.DataFrame({"look,comma": [  # same thing
    "wow, amazing",
    "wonderful"
]}).to_csv("foo.csv")
```

```bash
$ cat foo.csv
,"look,comma"     # looks OK!
0,"wow, amazing"
1,wonderful
```


Steps To Reproduce:

See above.

issue_title: update minimum numpy to 1.10.1
issue_body:
I'd like to upgrade the minimum supported numpy version to 1.10.1. This numpy version was released in Oct 2015.

Numpy 1.10 added two features that are useful to us:

1. nan support in ``assert_allclose``
2. scalar support in ``np.digitize``

We've hacked around 1. in the test suite for awhile now, but it would be great to remove the hacked code.

#429 proposes using ``np.digitize`` to replace manual binning in the ``perez`` function. I could hack around this too, but I'd rather bump the numpy spec.

Any objections to bumping the numpy spec?

issue_title: Make SpecError alias to ValueError
issue_body:
Originally reported by: **Daniel Plachotich (Bitbucket: [danpla](http://bitbucket.org/danpla), GitHub: [danpla](http://github.com/danpla))**

----------------------------------------

The biggest advantage of this is that read() methods will able to call validate() and encode() without catching ValueError and re-raising SpecError or the code duplication. The code of some specs will be much better.

Currently, SpecError means that the value of the `data` was invalid — this is semantically equivalently to ValueError. Despite the fact that the SpecError currently used only internally, it can be left as an alias to ValueError if someone uses it externally for some reason, since Mutagen imports all the guts to the public interface.

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/lazka/mutagen/issue/243

issue_title: APIC Tag Uses Image Description
issue_body:
Hello, I just wanted to say, it seems to me the way APIC keys are handled in mutagen 1.36.2 is not a very good approach. Creating keys of the form `APIC:<image-description>` does not seem like a good idea since there is no guarantee that a user's music library has standardized image descriptions. Couldn't we use the fact we know if an image is a front cover to create a key like `APIC:Cover_Front` or whatever type the image is?

This comes up because I am writing some scripts to deal with my music library, and some of my files have front covers with descriptions saying "Front Cover" and others are the name of the file that was added as the front cover. I would like to be able to just say I want all the front covers. And this should be possible since that information is included in APIC tags, correct?

https://travis-ci.org/Z3Prover/z3 currently says "This is not an active repository" yet we currently have working builds.

A quick google search shows other people have hit this

https://stackoverflow.com/questions/32022899/seeing-this-is-not-an-active-repository-for-an-active-repository

It sounds like the TravisCI hooks was manually enable from inside github rather than enabling it from within TravisCI. This likely means we don't have GitHub integration properly setup.

@NikolajBjorner @wintersteiger 

Based on the response in the stackoverflow post I think one of you need to go to https://travis-ci.org/ , click "sign in with GitHub" if not already signed in and then go to your profile (i.e. https://travis-ci.org/profile/NikolaBjorner or https://travis-ci.org/profile/wintersteiger ) and manually enable TravisCI for the `Z3Prover/z3` repository.

issue_title: Support exactly-k-out-of-N constraints
issue_body:
Klieber & Kwon show a nice & simple formulation in "Efficient CNF Encoding for Selecting 1 from N Objects", 2007 (https://www.cs.cmu.edu/~wklieber/papers/2007_efficient-cnf-encoding-for-selecting-1.pdf) of k-of-N constraints.

At the moment I work by combining AND & AtMost constraints, which lead to "meh" performance. Perhaps a better formulation would help, and a nice interface for the above-mentioned algorithm would make it so much easier for users.

issue_title: Z3 returns an incorrect model
issue_body:
I tried the following example with the latest source code as well as the recent nightly build  z3-4.4.2.5d9820f3e2d4 of Z3

    (declare-fun n () Int)
    (declare-fun a () Int)
    (declare-fun b () Int)
    (declare-fun c () Int)
    (declare-fun l1 () Int)
    (declare-fun l2 () Int)
    (declare-fun l3 () Int)
    (declare-fun l4 () Int)

    (define-fun absolute ((x Int)) Int
        (ite (>= x 0) x (- x)))

    (assert (>= l1 0))
    (assert (>= l2 0))
    (assert (>= l3 0))
    (assert (>= l4 0))

    ;; |a| + |b| > 0
    (assert (> (+ (absolute a) (absolute b)) 0)) 

    ;; -l1 + l2 + a*l3 = 0
    (assert (= (+ (+ (- 0 l1) l2) (* a l3)) 0))
    ;; l1 - l2 + b*l3 = 0
    (assert (= (+ (+ l1 (- 0 l2)) (* b l3)) 0))
    ;; c*l3 + l4 = 0
    (assert (= (+ (+ (* c l3) l4) 1) 0))

    (minimize (+ (+ (absolute a) (absolute b)) (absolute c)))
    (check-sat)
    (get-model)

and the returned model is

    (a, -1), (b, 0), (c, -1), (l1, 0), (l2, 0), (l3, 1), (l4, 0)

which, I think, is incorrect because the model does not satisfy the assertion

    -l1 + l2 + a*l3 = 0

A possible model is

    (a, 1), (b, -1), (c, -1), (l1, 1), (l2, 0), (l3, 1), (l4, 0)

Could you please help to investigate the problem? Thank you.
