When working with natural language processing or multiclassification with a lot of classes, one frequently uses hierarchical or sampled softmax functions when predicting class probabilities.

Theano already has a `T.nnet.h_softmax` function, but it takes freaking 9 inputs most of which can be inferred.

Do we need a layer like [this](https://gist.github.com/justheuristic/581853c6d6b87eae9669297c2fb1052d) to allow users to simply plug h_softmax in.

Defaults to n_classes=sqrt(n_outputs).

If we do need it, feel free to use the code in any way you want.

issue_title: The potato problem: pluralizing words in MultiContainerInterfaces
issue_body:
`MultiContainerInterface` attempts to pluralize the term for the objects it contains, however it does not always do this correctly. As a result, a user must use the incorrect pluralization of some words in order to get a `MultiContainerInterface` extension to work. See [extensions tutorial](https://pynwb.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tutorials/general/extensions.html#creating-and-using-a-custom-multicontainerinterface) and #585. I'm not sure where this behavior is defined. 

English morphology is irregular and difficult, and it will be hard to get this right every time without adding unnecessary dependencies or complicated code. I propose that we stop attempting to pluralize words and just use the singular form as they are entered. Another option that could work would be to have the user explicitly enter the plural form of the word they wish to use instead of attempting to automatically pluralize the word.

issue_title: nteract as a quiver replacement
issue_body:
On macOS, I use a program called [quiver](hhttp://happenapps.com/#quiver), which promotes itself as "The Programmer's Notebook".  It has a number of features that make it rather nice for my day to day work. However, it is a macOS only application.  I recently came across nteract and tried it out. I think that nteract (with its ability to run code) could become an upgrade to quiver, if it took on a few features.

Vocabulary: What quiver calls a note is what nteract or jupyter would call a notebook.  A note supports rich text, markdown, latex, and code cells.  What quiver calls a notebook is more like what most apps would call a folder.

Features that make quiver my daily driver:

**Auto-save:**

All notes/notebooks are saved in one location, automatically. As soon as I create a note, it is saved. I don't have to select a location on my file system and give the note a name. If something comes up, I just create a new note. Every note I make is automatically saved in the "inbox" folder, and I can move it to another folder to categorize. I can also add tags to these notes.  I do a lot of "work logs" in markdown cells, and I put code snippets in all over the place. When I open quiver, all of the notes I've created are listed and available. 

Switching to having a single save directory might be a negative for some people ("I want to store my notebook with the larger project I'm working on!"), but I think the automatic saving and collection will be a welcome addition for a lot of people. As long as you can still save individual notebooks in custom directories, no functionality would be lost.

**Organizational Columns**

This goes along with auto-saving in one location. Quiver has a list of "notebooks" and "notes" on the left, and you can sort them by title, creation date, or updated. 

**Searching:** 

For all the notes that quiver knows about, it can do a full-text search of them. If nteract would default save all of its notebooks into a common location, it could perform searches across them. 

**Global Hot Key**

This might be difficult to implement across multiple platforms, but I have a key combo to enter a single note, and another one to bring up the entire collection of notes.

issue_title: factory.call_state API is hard to use
issue_body:
I tried to use `factory.call_state`, but I hit several problems:

* it is not possible to specify stack alignment requirements
* the current API is very "ad hoc", and does surprising things. For example:
    * anything that has length 4 is endian-adjusted. so if I use a bitvector for a string of length 4 it will reverse the string
    * strings get automatically converted to pointers, but `BVS` / `BVV` longer than 4 isn't
    * strings are auomatically null terminated

In general, I find it very hard to understand how `factory.call_state` (and `SimCC.setup_callsite`) will convert my values. Can this API be redesigned? I would volunteer to attempt this, what is the policy on backwards compatibility?

I am using a fresh: v601r23 version and I ma getting the following error:

In the ganga submission script I did define the following:

j.outputfiles=[MassStorageFile(namePattern="*.root", outputfilenameformat=project+'/'+unique_production_name+'/{jid}/{sjid}/{fname}’)]

I think all looks OK and the files are at least stored on the desired eos location.

Can you please advise what to do?
```

The script used for this is:

```
import os

project = 'DiMuon'
configfile = "DiMuon4.py"

unique_production_name = '2011_21r1p1a_EW_up'

# for testing

projectpath = os.environ['HOME']+'/workspace/LHCb/'+project+'/'

j=Job(application=DaVinci( version = "v39r1p1" ), backend=Dirac() )

j.name = unique_production_name
print "\n["+j.name+"] Working on: " + j.name

print "\n["+j.name+"] Initalizing ganga running of project: " + project + " with unique_production_name version: " + unique_production_name
print "["+j.name+"] Using project path: "+ projectpath +"\n"

print "["+j.name+"] Config file: " + configfile
print "["+j.name+"] Data list in file/from query:\n"
print datalist
print "\n"

j.application.optsfile = [ File ( projectpath+configfile ) ]

j.application.extraopts="""
DaVinci().PrintFreq = 1000
DaVinci().EvtMax = -1
DaVinci().InputType = 'DST'
DaVinci().DataType = '2011'
DaVinci().Simulation = False
"""
print "\n["+j.name+"] Here are the changed job options: "
print j.application.extraopts

#j.inputdata = j.application.readInputData(projectpath+datalist )
j.inputdata = datalist

j.splitter = SplitByFiles( filesPerJob = 10 )
j.parallel_submit = True
j.outputfiles=[MassStorageFile(namePattern="*.root", outputfilenameformat=project+'/'+unique_production_name+'/{jid}/{sjid}/{fname}')]
#j.outputfiles=[DiracFile(DiracFile(namePattern='*.root',locations=["CERN-USER"])]

j.submit()

(This functionality should also exist across all IGangaFile object types eventually!)

issue_title: Deleting a collection doesn't delete access_control_entries for its children
issue_body: `buckets.py` has an event listener that ensures that when a bucket is deleted, everything underneath it is recursively deleted. `collections.py` has one too but it doesn't appear to be as robust -- it doesn't have a wildcard to match objects more than one level below it (which might be OK, since only records are below collections now), and `delete_object_permissions` is only called on the collection rather than its children.

Hi,

My D415 camera is attached to a manipulator arm robot, so I had to use a 5 meter cable to connect the camera to my PC. I'm using two USB 3.0 cables of 3m and 2m (in addition to the the small cable that came with the camera).

I work with Python, and I tried to reduce the size of the data from the camera by disabling the streams that I'm not using (I only use the depth stream in the highest resolution) and increasing the waiting time for a new frame to arrive. Here's the code:

    config = rs.config()

issue_title: Reset Pulp Server prior to running Pulp Smash during an upgrade test
issue_body:
Our upgrade jobs use the following steps:

1. Install $OLDER version of Pulp
1. [Populate](https://github.com/pulp/pulp_packaging/blob/master/ci/jobs/scripts/pre-upgrade.sh) the server with random content
1. Upgrade the server to $LATEST version of Pulp
1. Run Pulp Smash test suite

By the time we run the automation, the server will already have some data present which can potentially lead to some false negative results in the automation (such as orphan repos or packages).

What we want to do is make sure that we reset the server prior to running the automation but only after we have performed a post-upgrade test. (see [517](https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/issues/517)

issue_title: Test dependency solving resolution on RPM repo copy
issue_body:
When recursively copying rpm units from one repo to another, only the latest units should be copied. The zoo repo provides packages with dependencies that can be used to verify this functionality. Specifically, it provides two version of 'walrus', and 'chimpanzee' depends on walrus; only one version of walrus should be copied when recursively copying chimpanzee.

Here are the relevant pulp-admin commands:
```
pulp-admin rpm repo create --repo-id zoo --feed https://repos.fedorapeople.org/repos/pulp/pulp/demo_repos/zoo/
pulp-admin rpm repo sync run --repo-id zoo
pulp-admin rpm repo create --repo-id new
pulp-admin rpm repo copy rpm --from-repo-id zoo --to-repo-id new --str-eq="name=chimpanzee --recursive"
```

If this is working correctly, only walrus 5.21 is associated with the 'new' repository. If this is broken, both walrus 0.71 and walrus 5.21 will be associated with the 'new' repository.

This test could potentially be used to test dependency solving generally (or perhaps this could be a separate issue). If it's working correctly, the only latest versions of  camel, chimpanzee, fox, squirrel, and walrus will be in the 'new' repo. If the number of packages associated with the 'new' repo after copy is not exactly 5, and the names aren't the 5 aforementioned names, dependency solving is generally broken.

More information can be found in https://pulp.plan.io/issues/1432#note-9.

DroneDeploy has recently released our [GraphQL APIs](https://developer.dronedeploy.com/reference/) on top of graphene and tornado. As part of this, we ported `graphene-django` over to tornado and have made it [available](https://github.com/dronedeploy/graphene-tornado/) as open source.

issue_title: Using Unions in a Connection
Execute should return an awaitable with AsyncioExecutor
issue_body:
The current graphql.execution.execute.execute implementation forces AsyncioExecutor to use loop.run_until_complete in wait_until_finished. This is a bit ugly.

It would be better to let the executor decide what is returned from the execute method. It can be given a function to call after the data is ready as an argument.

With this AsyncioExecutor could return an awaitable and execute would be used as one would expect

`result = await execute(schema, ast, executor=AsyncioExecutor())`

It could even have an argument to get the new way to keep compatibility with existing uses.

This would also make TwistedExecutor much easier to implement.

issue_title: Documentation & GH Pages
graphene_django can't be imported without configuring Django settings
issue_body:
When starting up a fresh Python interpreter, merely importing `graphene_django` raises an exception:

```
>>> import graphene_django
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
django.core.exceptions.ImproperlyConfigured: Requested setting DEBUG, but settings are not configured. You must either define the environment variable DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE or call settings.configure() before accessing settings.
```

While this doesn't seem like that big a deal (and in the grand scheme of things, it really isn't), it does break a few unexpected things.  For example, when I ran into some confusing things in the documentation (see #499), I tried examining the source using [pipenv](https://pipenv.readthedocs.io/en/latest/):

```bash
$ pipenv open graphene_django
Module not found!
```

This was super confusing, and admittedly might really be a bug in pipenv, but I assume it thought the module wasn't found because of the `ImproperlyConfigured` exception raised by importing `graphene_django`.

Anyways, if it's easy to change the package so that its root can be imported without requiring Django settings to be initialized, that would be helpful, but it's probably not really that big a deal if not.

issue_title: has_previous_page and has_next_page always False while navigating
issue_body:
While using the pageInfo hasPreviousPage and hasNextPage while navigation before and after cursors it seems these results are always set to False.

The corresponding code (https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-relay-py/blob/master/graphql_relay/connection/arrayconnection.py#L104-L105):
```python
return connection_type(
    edges=edges,
    page_info=pageinfo_type(
        start_cursor=first_edge_cursor,
        end_cursor=last_edge_cursor,
        has_previous_page=isinstance(last, int) and start_offset > lower_bound,
        has_next_page=isinstance(first, int) and end_offset < upper_bound
    )
)
```

Querying with `first: 5, after: "xxx"` in this case always results in `hasPreviousPage: false`.
The same for `last: 5, before: "xxx"`, which results always in `hasNextPage: false`.

issue_body:
[improvement]

Currently `debugsqlshell` management command is basically Django's built-in `shell` command + extra options that debug toolbar provides. 

I think it would be great if debug toolbar used `django-extensions`' `shell_plus` command instead of vanilla django shell **or** had another management command (like `debugsqlshell_plus`) that used shell plus.

It isn't really hard to implement in the first sight; just changing

```
from django.core.management.commands.shell import Command  # noqa
```

to
```
try:
    from django_extensions.managemen.commands.shell_plus import Command # noqa
except ImportError:
    # In case django extensions is not installed 
    from django.core.management.commands.shell import Command  # noqa
```

will do.


I think adding this feature is just a matter of your decision.

issue_title: NRF52840 build fails on GCC 7.2 -Wexpansion-to-defined
issue_body: On a clean git clone on a fresh install of Ubuntu 17.10, the build environment bootstrap script will install the latest arm-none-eabi-gcc, version 7.2.1. GCC version 7 includes `-Wexpansion-to-defined`, which causes multiple macros in the Nordic libraries to throw warnings, which are treated as errors. This can be fixed by adding `-Wno-expansion-to-defined` to the `COMMONCFLAGS` variable in `Makefile-nrf52840`. I am working on a pull request to add this flag conditionally based on installed GCC version.

- code coverage: how to run analysis; how to view html output?
- doxygen: how to regenerate the docs?
- check: how to run certification tests; how to configure to run against hardware vs. simulator?

- cross-compilation: how to compile the library for a different target architecture?

issue_title: GKE authentication
issue_body:
pykube is not capable of authenticating against GKE clusters. Recent clusters on GKE use Google OAuth to authenticate. The user configuration in `~/.kube/config` looks like:

```
user:
  auth-provider:
    name: gcp
```

Once `kubectl` runs with that configuration it adds in the `config` key with an `access-token` and `expiry`.

In my initial research, I learned that oauth2client uses httplib which is a non-starter for integrating it with pykube. However, there's hope with https://github.com/google/oauth2client/issues/128

issue_title: bug in parsing apache conf with named regex backreferences, fails with "There has been an error in parsing the file"
issue_body:
I'm on Ubuntu 16.04.1 with the deb letsencrypt 0.4.1-1. I have a section in my apache `mydomain.com-ssl.conf` as
```
<LocationMatch ^/ec/(?<userid>foo|bar)>
  Require user %{env:MATCH_USERID}
</LocationMatch>
```
This is perfectly [valid](https://httpd.apache.org/docs/2.4/mod/core.html#locationmatch) (as of apache 2.4.8) and working just fine, but letsencrypt chokes with
```
The apache plugin is not working; there may be problems with your existing configuration.
The error was: PluginError(('There has been an error in parsing the file (%s): %s', u'/etc/apache2/sites-enabled/mydomain.com-ssl.conf', u'Syntax error'),)
```
Commenting out the section makes letsencrypt happy.

```

issue_title: Nginx should give a prompt when it can't find a server_name
issue_body:
When the nginx plugin can't find a server name it doesn't give you any
advice on what to do. It should tell you to add a server_name directive
to your nginx config and hint at where that might be.

issue_title: Nginx plugin selection
issue_body:
We want to slowly roll out the Nginx plugin to make sure it's working for people. To do this we should:
* Mark the nginx plugin as hidden (we did this for the manual plugin)
* Disable automatic selection if it's the only available configurator (if not already the case, we should disable automatic selection of hidden plugins).
* Make sure the description of the Nginx plugin is sane (it used to say it was broken).

issue_title: Feature Request: enable simple frame compression without content-size header
issue_body:
The lz4 frame specification makes the content-size header field optional. I would love to be able to `lz4.frame.compress(foo, content_size=False)` and have the encoded frame not include the C.Size FLG and the optional 8 bytes of size data.

For example, the lz4f package does not include the content-size header and results in different bits:

```
In [1]: import lz4f
In [2]: lz4f.compressFrame(b'1234')
Out[2]: '\x04"M\x18`@\x82\x04\x00\x00\x801234\x00\x00\x00\x00'
In [3]: import lz4.frame
In [4]: lz4.frame.compress(b'1234')
Out[4]: '\x04"M\x18h@\x04\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\xcd\x04\x00\x00\x801234\x00\x00\x00\x00'
```

issue_title: Active markdown cells.
issue_body:
Currently markdown cells are handled at the frontend and does not interact with the kernel, so it does not support string interpolation. It would be nice to allow sos to handle markdown cells so that we can handle string interpolation. There are two solutions:

1. Hijack notebook markdown cells. We cannot hijack keyboard events since the notebook can be rendered in batch, so we have to change the way markdown cells are handled. This would require us to change the prototype of [`TextCell`](https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/blob/master/notebook/static/notebook/js/textcell.js), which is to my understanding very difficult.

2. Create a new "kernel" type named "md". Basically the regular execute mechanism will be used to send the text to the sos kernel. SoS would do something to it (string interpolation) and send the text back through the msg channel. The frontend accepts the text and render it to HTML in place of the cell. The latter part can be a bit difficult (hide input cell etc) but should be doable by following the TextCell way.

This can be useful but I am not sure how to translate this to batch mode sos script.

issue_title: Naming convention for path?
issue_body:
Just wondering if we should somehow notify sos the type of variable so that SoS knows what should be translated during remote execution, something like

```
run:
   echo  $(title) >> ${output}
```

or 

```
run:
   echo $${title} >> ${output}
```

or

```
run:
   echo $_{title} >> ${output}
```

instead of

```
task:   preserved_vars='title'
run:
    echo ${title} >> ${output}
```

Of course, `preserved_vars` is much easier to implement than such naming conventions.

issue_title: Confusing on parameter, definition, and global variable.
issue_body:
Suppose in notebook, we have

```
fname = 'a.txt'
```
already defined, then

```
%run --fname b.txt

input: fname
```
would actually take `a.txt` because `fname` is not defined as parameter, and a value already exists. We should either

1. stop using global variables ( #690 ) so that `fname` is undefined when it is not defined in `parameter`.
2. check for unused variable and notify user that `--fname b.txt` is not used because `fname` is not defined in `parameter`.

issue_title: Remote tasks submitted from a laptop
issue_body: I'm asking this on behalf of laptop users: submitting jobs directly from the HPC system is easier for laptop users because they can submit, logout, fold the laptop screen and move on to their next physical destination. With SoS it is a bit harder because we have to keep a process running to monitor jobs and rsync data. Is there any best practice recommendation for working with laptop type of setup? (in my office area there are many more laptops than desktops).

issue_title: SoS submitter hang on cluster
issue_body:
I've experienced various hanging behavior for the job submitter. "Hang" here means that job queue is empty but SoS refuses to move on. It looks like stuck on the current job submission, yet `ps -A | grep sos` shows nothing. With `ctrl-c` I can keyboard interrupt it.

There are now 2 types of hangs I can reliably reproduce. Hopefully by describing them you'll be able to make some MWE for your cluster:

1. When my job exceeds the `walltime`
2. When my specified directory for `err` and `out` files do not exist, eg:

```
      #SBATCH --output={cur_dir}/non_existing_dir/{job_name}.out
      #SBATCH --error={cur_dir}/non_existing_dir/{job_name}.err
```

I hope this is enough to reproduce it.

issue_title: AWS CloudWatch Log Streams Accumulate
issue_body:
Within AWS CloudWatch Log Groups, old Log Streams linger until they are pruned through an external process.  While I have not observed this to create any logging failures, I have some Log Groups over four thousand (4,000) empty Log Streams. The command `pulumi logs` appears to run much more slowly on the stacks with excessive log streams, and I hypothesize there to be a correlation.

Strangely, some of my current Log Streams report zero storedBytes, while AWS reports bytes stored on older streams that are outside the retention period. A bug in AWS would not shock me at this point. To at least validate the impact, I'm pruning some of the log groups in our test stack.

issue_title: [tune] Trainable functions cannot execute Ray methods since Ray is not thread safe
issue_body:
### Describe the problem

The following snippet will crash with

```
ray.tune.error.TuneError: Error running trial: The Ray methods are not thread safe and must be called from the main thread. This method was called from thread Thread-2.
```

```
import ray
from ray.tune import run_experiments, register_trainable


ray.init()

def my_train(config, reporter):  # add the reporter parameter
    ray.put("HI")

ray.tune.register_trainable("my_train", my_train)


ray.tune.run_experiments({
    "my_experiment": {
        "run": "my_train",
    }
})
```

This is since Tune has to run long-running functions off of the main thread. This prevents tune users from interacting with Ray when using the trainable functions interface.

One workaround is to define the trainable as a Trainable class, which tune does run on the main thread since train() yields control back to the tune periodically for returning results.

issue_title: Set a query ID
issue_body:
Clickhouse allows to set an ID on queries, you can see this IDs with a:
```
select query_id from system.processes;
```
this is useful when you want to cancel a query from an external process as explain here: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/40546983/how-to-kill-a-process-query-in-clickhouse

I propose that the `connection.send_query()` method accepts a string argument to name the query. It could be used in `client.process_*_query()` functions and `execute()` as an optional argument:

```
import uuid
c = client('server')
query_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
c.execute("select something", queryid=query_id)
```

- [X] Suggestion
- [ ] Bug

### Brief description of the problem
Currently, the maximum amount of characters allowed in all fields combined in a single help embed is 1000. [See this code](https://github.com/Cog-Creators/Red-DiscordBot/blob/V3/develop/redbot/core/help_formatter.py#L193).

I think this is way too little and causes the bot to split `[p]help` into multiple messages much too quickly. Instead, I think the limit should be increased to something like 2000. 
Technically, the maximum amount of characters a rich embed can have is 6000, but I can understand that it's not desirable for the embeds to be *that* big (especially when taking mobile users into consideration).
On the contrary, I think a limit of 1000 makes the bot unnecessarily split the fields too quickly. 2000 is a nice middle ground between the two.

TL;DR - Increase the limit of 1000 chars per help embed to something like 2000 chars per help embed.

issue_title: Usage of API and documentation for projection metadata in las files
issue_body:
I have hesitated to ask this question, because I don't know if this is the correct forum to ask questions that are mainly usage related. However, I have decided that this question is both an API issue and documentation issue so is relevant to ask here.

A common problem for people working on lidar datasets is rasterizing point clouds.  Documenting how to pull out the projection information from each las file version would make it much easier to write code that can rasterize a point cloud. 

This is a feature request to document how to easily obtain the metadata needed for using lidar data read by laspy that is required to produce a georeferenced raster using GDAL.  The main challenge I have run into is getting the projection from the las file using laspy.  I've yet to figure out how to do this with 1.0 las files that I know have projection metadata. 

More generally, is there any interest in having a user forum to discuss usage of laspy where both laspy users and developers can ask questions not directly related to development of laspy, but geared at laspy usage?  Laspy is a library that sits in a wonderful ecosystem of open source geospatial software that is well supported by Python developers.  The laspy documentation does not explain how to use laspy in the broader context of other geospatial libraries -- such as GDAL -- that can be used to rasterize a point cloud.

issue_title: Add group by support
issue_body:
I would like to use "group by" feature of SQLAlchemy.
My solution is based on sort feature already implemented.

## Solution
1. Parse group_by arg in find method of SQL class.
```
def extract_group_by_arg(req):
    group_by = req.args.get('group_by')
    if group_by:
        if re.match('^[,\w]+$', group_by):
            arg = []
            for s in group_by.split(','):
                arg.append(s)
            return arg
        else:
            return ast.literal_eval(group_by)
    else:
        return None

args = {'sort': extract_sort_arg(req),
                    'group_by': extract_group_by_arg(req),
                    'resource': resource}
```
2. Add parsed list to args param of SQLAResultCollection.
```
def parse_group_by(model, query, key):
    """
    Group by parser that works with embedded resources
    Moved out from the query (find) method.
    """
    if '.' in key:  # group by related mapper class
        rel, group_by_attr = key.split('.')
        rel_class = getattr(model, rel).property.mapper.class_
        query = query.outerjoin(rel_class)
        base = getattr(rel_class, group_by_attr)
    else:
        base = getattr(model, key)

    return base

if args['group_by']:
    ql = []
    for group_item in args['group_by']:
        ql.append(parse_group_by(model, query, group_item))
    args['group_by'] = ql
```
3. Use this list in order to use group_by SQLAlchemy method in _query attribute of SQLAResultCollection class.
```
self._group_by = kwargs.get('group_by')
if self._group_by:
    self._query = self._query.group_by(*self._group_by)
```

## Usage example

> /resource?group_by=name,age

issue_title: Consider changing our `block` terminology
issue_body:
I propose changing `taskBlock` and `nodes` to `tasks_per_node` and `nodes_per_block`. Instead of users having to learn: 
- Block concept
- Node concept
- Node parameter definition (node parameter = nodes / block)
- TaskBlock concept
- TaskBlock parameter definition (TaskBlock parameter = TaskBlocks / Block)
- Task concept

They would just have to learn:
- Block concept
- Node concept
- Task concept

The parameter definitions would be clear from the name. For example, with the current terminology, you would need to look at the illustrations in the docs to understand the definition. On the other hand, I think most people could draw the corresponding diagram given the names `tasks_per_node` and `nodes_per_block`.

Example 1
----------
**current**
```
"block": {
    "taskBlocks": 4,
    "nodes": 1
}
```

**proposed**
```
"block": {
    "nodes_per_block": 1,
    "tasks_per_node": 4
}
```
![n1_t4](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5114833/40025382-ff02696c-5796-11e8-9d42-abccd6803ba5.png)


Example 2
----------

**current**
```
"block": {
    "taskBlocks": 2,
    "nodes": 4
}
```

**proposed**
```
"block": {
    "nodes_per_block": 4,
    "tasks_per_node": 1 / 2.
}
```
![n4_t2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5114833/40025377-fd62435c-5796-11e8-98d6-281d5661a59d.png)

issue_title: mprof disables usage of __file__
issue_body:
When running any script with `mprof` the `__file__` in profiled program is always is path to `memory_profiler.py`.

## Example

Lets say that you profile script that reads some data file from relative location specified using `__file__`

```
# script_to_profile.py
import os
data_path = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), data.csv)
print(data_path)
```

executing this as `python <path/to/script/>script_to_profile.py` 
prints `<path/to/script/>data.csv`

however, running `mprof run <path/to/script/>script_to_profile.py`
prints `<path/to/site-packages/>data.csv`

this makes it impossible to profile scripts that rely on specifying paths to static files using __file__

issue_title: Duplicate jobs getting scheduled with croniter 0.3.22
issue_body:
It looks like `croniter 0.3.22` was released very recently. With that version, `croniter.get_next()` API behavior changed (looks like a bug) compared to `0.3.20` version. See https://github.com/taichino/croniter/issues/102.

Due to above croniter issue, if scheduler is running with less than 60 seconds interval, then there is possibility that scheduler incorrectly schedules "cron" based same job multiple times.

As an example, with croniter 0.3.22, let's say the job has cron configuration of `'0,30 * * * *'` (i.e. job is run at every 0 and 30 minutes). And let's say scheduler interval is 30 seconds. In that case:

```
Scheduler Run Time    | Scheduler Ran The Job?   | Next Job Schedule Time
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
08:58:40              | No                       | 09:00:00
08:59:10              | No                       | 09:00:00
08:59:40              | No                       | 09:00:00
09:00:10              | Yes                      | 09:00:00 .  <- Since croniter.get_next() returns 09:00:00, job gets scheduled again at 9:00:00 incorrectly
09:00:40              | Yes                      | 09:00:00 .  <- Since croniter.get_next() returns 09:00:00, job gets scheduled again at 9:00:00 incorrectly
09:01:10              | Yes                      | 09:30:00
09:01:40              | No                       | 09:30:00
```

Hi. I'm trying to run Splinter in a Windows 7 machine, in a local network that needs a proxy. When a new instance of the Browser() is created in the python shell, a firefox window pop-ups with the profile different than the one that I use. So, this new instance and profile don't have none of the configs for proxy. This is the message generated by the "browser = Browser()" code: Traceback (most recent call last): File "C:\Users\cassio.nandi\Desktop\splinter\exemplo.py", line 3, in browser = Browser() File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\splinter rowser.py", line 46, in Browser return driver(*args, **kwargs) File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\splinter\driver\webdriver irefox.py", line 23, in __init__ self.driver = Firefox(firefox_profile) File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\selenium\webdriver irefox\webdriver.py", line 47, in __init__ desired_capabilities=DesiredCapabilities.FIREFOX) File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\selenium\webdriver emote\webdriver.py", line 61, in __init__ self.start_session(desired_capabilities, browser_profile) File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\selenium\webdriver emote\webdriver.py", line 98, in start_session 'desiredCapabilities': desired_capabilities, File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\selenium\webdriver emote\webdriver.py", line 144, in execute self.error_handler.check_response(response) File "C:\Python27\lib\site-packages\selenium\webdriver emote\errorhandler.py", line 100, in check_response raise exception_class(value) WebDriverException: Message: ' ERRO: A URL solicitada não pode ser recuperada

issue_title: Please update the CA bundle shipped with this python library
issue_body: Please update the CA bundle shipped with this python library. Since June 2011 some CA's have been removed from the 'default ubuntu CA bundle'. ( https://github.com/stripe/stripe-python/blob/master/stripe/data/ca-certificates.crt actually reports the date of the CA bundle to be May 9, 2011 ).

issue_title: serialize(format="pretty-xml") fails on cyclic links
issue_body:
jman...@gmail.com, 2011-08-13T00:28:24.000Z 
When cyclically related nodes are serialized with format="pretty-xml", the nodeID for the outermost node is not included.  This leaves the inner node making a dangling (unresolved) reference.  See below for details...

--------------
What steps will reproduce the problem?

  $ [Fresh Ubuntu 11.04 install]
  $ sudo easy_install -U "rdflib>=3.0.0"

  $ python

from rdflib import *
g = Graph()
g.bind("j", URIRef("http://example.com#"))

g.add((a, RDF.type, URIRef("http://example.com#a")))
g.add((b, RDF.type, URIRef("http://example.com#b")))

g.add((a, URIRef("htt://example.com#linkedto"), b))
g.add((b, URIRef("htt://example.com#linkedto"), a))

print g.serialize(format="pretty-xml")

--------------
What is the expected output? 



  
    
      
        
      
    
  



--------------
What do you see instead?

The node of type  does *not* have a nodeID assigned, meaning that the reference is broken!  Exact output below:



  
    
      
        
      
    
  


--------------
What version of the product are you using? On what operating system?

  $ [Fresh Ubuntu 11.04 install]
  $ sudo easy_install -U "rdflib>=3.0.0"

Comment 1 by gjhigg...@gmail.com
Good catch. The PrettyXMLSerializer code is explicit about this (mis)behaviour:

if isinstance(subject, BNode):
    def subj_as_obj_more_than(ceil):
        return more_than(store.triples((None, None, subject)), ceil)
    #here we only include BNode labels if they are referenced
    #more than once (this reduces the use of redundant BNode identifiers)
    if subj_as_obj_more_than(1):
        writer.attribute(RDF.nodeID, fix(subject))

and this produces exactly the disconnect that you are seeing.

Just to confirm: this issue is restricted to the output of PrettyXMLSerializer - choose any serialization other than pretty-xml and you will be able to parse it back into a properly-connected graph.

IMO, it's a false economy and could usefully be dispensed with.


Comment 2 by gjhigg...@gmail.com
Fixed in changeset 3ddb26ab8f4f - check for BNode references > 1 commented out.

issue_title: easy_install fails with conflicting paver-minilib.zip files
issue_body:
If paver-minilib.zip is generated for different dependant packages with different paver version
then easy_install can fail.
Example: https://github.com/ponty/PyVirtualDisplay/issues/5

Possible workarounds:
 - using pip instead easy_install
 - calling easy_install more times: first fails, second succeeds
 - uploading all package with latest paver-minilib.zip
